nativeRange.habitat: Found on outer lagoon and seaward reef sand slopes, typically on patches or expanses of coarse carbonate sand at depths of roughly 10–40 m.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of frozen and prepared meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and finely chopped table shrimp. Feeding twice daily is commonly advised.

avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp (may be threatened by this goby)

imageCandidates.notes: Sushi Girl1995, 2010x2158. Striking aquarium shot with dorsal/anal fins fully spread; vibrant yellow-orange spots ringed with blue, dark body bars and blackish pelvic fins all clearly shown. Sharp, whole fish in frame.
Rickard Zerpe, 1958x1469. Sharp in-situ image at burrow entrance on reef sand; orange-yellow spots and dark pelvic fin visible, showing natural habitat behaviour.

Elias Levy, 2748x1832. Goby alongside its yellow alpheid (pistol) shrimp partner on coral sand, illustrating the species' signature symbiotic burrow-sharing. Fish is smaller in frame but sharp and correctly ID'd.

summary: The Orange Spotted Goby is a small, peaceful shrimp-goby from the Western Pacific known for forming symbiotic partnerships with pistol shrimp, sharing a burrow in sandy reef substrate. Generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Orange Spotted Goby (Amblyeleotris guttata) is a burrowing goby native to sandy outer reef zones of the Western Pacific, ranging from the Philippines and Ryukyu Islands south to Australia and east to Tonga. It tends to be found at depths of roughly 10–40 m on coarse carbonate sand patches near reef slopes.

In the aquarium it generally does well with a sand substrate of at least a few centimetres depth and some loose coral rubble for shelter. One of the species' most appealing traits is its tendency to form symbiotic partnerships with alpheid shrimp, commonly known as pistol shrimp — the shrimp excavates and maintains the burrow while the goby perches at the entrance as a lookout, and both animals retreat to safety when the goby signals danger. This behaviour can be replicated in a well-set-up reef tank by introducing a compatible pistol shrimp alongside the goby.

A tight-fitting, well-sealed lid is strongly advised: like most gobies, this species has been known to jump from open aquariums.

Adult coloration is pale whitish-grey with 4–5 dark brown to black bars on the body, and distinctive bright orange spots covering the head and body. The pelvic fins are notably blackish.

The species is generally considered reef-safe, but has been noted to pose a potential threat to very small, delicate ornamental shrimp other than its alpheid partner. Larger ornamental shrimp are typically unaffected.
breedingNotes: Spawning has been reported in captivity. Like other shrimp gobies, the Orange Spotted Goby has been documented depositing eggs inside its burrow, with the male observed guarding the nest. Detailed captive-breeding protocols are not widely established in the hobby.

FishBase did not provide explicit pH or salinity SG values; marine standard applied.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has been reported in well-maintained reef aquariums. Eggs are typically deposited inside the burrow and guarded by the male. Raising larvae to settlement is considered difficult; detailed captive-breeding protocols are not widely established in the hobby.
